metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC #5 to explicitly assert that each new metric id (`module_size_distribution`, `churn`, `cap_change_events`, `refactor_to_addition_ratio`) appears in `REGISTRY` (e.g. `assert any(s.id == 'churn' for s in REGISTRY)`), not just that `is_authoritative('git')` is True.
evidence: AC #5: 'the metrics register into src/rebar/metrics/registry.py with source="git" and are authoritative (is_authoritative("git") is True) — asserted'
registry.py line 45: `AUTHORITATIVE_SOURCES: frozenset[str] = frozenset({"structural", "git", "sidecar", "snapshot"})` — git already present
No search hit for 'module_size_distribution' or 'cap_change_events' or 'churn' in REGISTRY — the new ids are absent from existing code, and AC #5 does not assert their presence
findings.finding: AC #5 asserts `is_authoritative("git")` is True as evidence that the metrics are correctly registered. However, `is_authoritative` checks only the `source` string against `AUTHORITATIVE_SOURCES` — `'git'` is already in that frozenset in the existing code, so this assertion passes trivially even if no new metrics are registered at all. The AC does not assert that the new metric ids (`module_size_distribution`, `churn`, `cap_change_events`, `refactor_to_addition_ratio`) actually appear in `REGISTRY`.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC #5
scenarios: An implementation that adds the functions to git_metrics.py but forgets to add the MetricSpec entries to REGISTRY would still pass AC #5 as written.
Registration completeness goes unverified — a partial implementation (only some metrics registered) would not be caught.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC: 'the REGISTRY contains a MetricSpec with id in {"module_size_distribution", "churn", "cap_change_events", "refactor_to_addition_ratio"} — each asserted by iterating REGISTRY and checking ids'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `cap_change_events` must use `git log -p -- .github/module-size-limit.txt` (and parse the unified diff for the numeric value) **or** pair `git log -- <file>` with per-commit `git show <sha>^:<file>` / `git show <sha>:<file>` calls to recover `old_limit` and `new_limit` — plain `git log` does not emit file content.
evidence: Plan quote: '`git log` over `.github/module-size-limit.txt` in range → list of `(commit, old_limit, new_limit)`'
`git log` output format: commit SHA, author, date, message — no file-content delta unless `-p` is added

Partially-verified: `git show refs/remotes/gh-main:"$limitfile"` is used in gerrit-verify.yaml line 187 to read a specific blob — confirming `git show <ref>:<path>` is the correct mechanism, not `git log`
EVIDENCE CLASS: Partially-verified (git log exists; the specific surface of extracting old/new values from it is Contradicted without -p or additional git show calls)
findings.finding: `cap_change_events` specifies that `git log` over `.github/module-size-limit.txt` yields `(commit, old_limit, new_limit)` tuples, but `git log` (even with `-- <path>`) only surfaces commit SHAs, messages, and timestamps — it does NOT emit file content before and after the change. Recovering `old_limit` and `new_limit` requires either `git log -p` (patch output) with diff parsing, or per-commit `git show <sha>^:<file>` / `git show <sha>:<file>` calls. The plan's described approach implies a capability that `git log` alone does not provide.
findings.location: Files section — `cap_change_events(repo_root, since, until)` description; also AC line 4
scenarios: Implementer runs `git log --follow -- .github/module-size-limit.txt` and gets only commit SHAs; cannot populate `old_limit`/`new_limit` without additional git commands not named in the plan.
AC line 4 test asserts `old` and `new` limit values are returned correctly — this test will fail unless the implementation uses `git show` or `git log -p`, neither of which the plan names.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Files section, replace 'git log over .github/module-size-limit.txt in range → list of (commit, old_limit, new_limit)' with: 'git log --format=%H -- .github/module-size-limit.txt to enumerate touching commits, then for each SHA recover old_limit = git show <sha>^:.github/module-size-limit.txt and new_limit = git show <sha>:.github/module-size-limit.txt; handle the first-commit case where sha^ does not exist (old_limit = None).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the multi-argument git-metric functions (`repo_root`, `ref`, `since`, `until`) are adapted to the registry's single-argument `compute: Callable[[Any], Any]` contract — e.g. via `functools.partial`, a wrapper lambda, or a typed context object — and reflect that in both the Files section and AC-5.
evidence: Plan: 'registered into src/rebar/metrics/registry.py'

Plan function signatures: 'module_size_distribution(repo_root, ref="HEAD")', 'cap_change_events(repo_root, since, until)', 'churn(repo_root, since, until)', 'refactor_to_addition_ratio(repo_root, since, until)'
registry.py:61: `compute: Callable[[Any], Any]` — verified single-argument contract
registry.py:111: `result = spec.compute(context)` — called with exactly one positional argument
No plan text describes partial application, wrapper, or context-dict to adapt multi-arg signatures to the single-arg contract
findings.finding: The plan asserts the multi-argument functions (`module_size_distribution(repo_root, ref="HEAD")`, `cap_change_events(repo_root, since, until)`, `churn(repo_root, since, until)`, `refactor_to_addition_ratio(repo_root, since, until)`) are 'registered into `src/rebar/metrics/registry.py`', but the verified `MetricSpec.compute` field is typed as `Callable[[Any], Any]` — a single-argument callable. The plan provides no bridging mechanism (e.g. `functools.partial`, wrapper lambda, or context-dict unpacking) to reconcile these multi-argument signatures with the single-argument registry contract.
findings.location: Files section ('registered into src/rebar/metrics/registry.py') and AC line 5 ('the metrics register into src/rebar/metrics/registry.py')
scenarios: Implementer registers `module_size_distribution` directly as `compute` and the registry calls it with one argument, causing a TypeError at evaluation time.
Implementer wraps each function in a lambda but the plan's AC asserts no specific wrapping contract, leaving the adapter shape undefined and untested.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: 'Each function is registered via `functools.partial(fn, repo_root=repo_root)` or a `context` dataclass carrying `repo_root`/`since`/`until`, so `compute(context)` satisfies the single-argument `MetricSpec` contract.' Update AC-5 to assert the specific adapter shape used.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how multi-argument git-metric functions are adapted to the single-context `MetricSpec.compute` contract (e.g. via `functools.partial`, a context dataclass, or a lambda) so the registration pattern is unambiguous.
evidence: Plan: 'module_size_distribution(repo_root, ref="HEAD")' / 'churn(repo_root, since, until)' / 'cap_change_events(repo_root, since, until)'
registry.py line 61: `compute: Callable[[Any], Any]` — single context argument
registry.py lines 111-112: `result = spec.compute(context)` — called with exactly one positional arg
Plan: 'registered into src/rebar/metrics/registry.py' — no mention of wrapping
findings.finding: The plan's named compute functions (`module_size_distribution(repo_root, ref)`, `churn(repo_root, since, until)`, `cap_change_events(repo_root, since, until)`, `refactor_to_addition_ratio(repo_root, since, until)`) have multi-argument signatures incompatible with the `MetricSpec.compute: Callable[[context], value | None]` contract in the existing registry. The plan does not specify how these functions are adapted (e.g. via `functools.partial` or lambda wrappers) when registered as `MetricSpec.compute` callables.
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py` function signatures; `src/rebar/metrics/registry.py` MetricSpec.compute contract
scenarios: If the functions are registered directly as compute callables, calling spec.compute(context) will raise TypeError (missing required positional arguments repo_root, since, until, etc.) at evaluation time.
The plan's AC tests for exact return values but don't specify how context supplies repo_root/since/until, leaving implementors without a clear contract.
findings.suggested_fix: Define a `GitMetricContext(repo_root, since=None, until=None, ref='HEAD')` dataclass and have each compute callable accept it as its single `context` argument; or note that registration uses `functools.partial(module_size_distribution, repo_root=..., ref='HEAD')` with `context` unused.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact git command sequence for extracting `old_limit` in `cap_change_events` (e.g., `git show <parent_sha>:.github/module-size-limit.txt` for each commit in the log range) and define the sentinel/convention for the file-creation case where no parent exists.
evidence: Plan says: 'git log over .github/module-size-limit.txt in range → list of (commit, old_limit, new_limit)'
ABSENCE: no specification of the parent-diff command (`git show <parent>:.github/module-size-limit.txt`) or diff-parsing strategy needed to extract `old_limit`
ABSENCE: no handling specified for the degenerate case where the file is created (no parent commit), so `old_limit` is undefined
findings.finding: `cap_change_events` specifies returning `(commit, old_limit, new_limit)` tuples but does not specify the mechanism for recovering `old_limit` — `git log` over a file gives commit SHAs and (with `-p`) diff hunks, but a naive implementation will only see the new value; and the very first commit that creates the file has no parent, making `old_limit` undefined.
findings.location: Files section – `cap_change_events(repo_root, since, until)` description
scenarios: Implementer uses `git log --follow -- .github/module-size-limit.txt` and only gets SHAs, producing tuples with `old_limit=None` for every entry.
The file is first committed (old_limit has no meaningful value) — the plan provides no sentinel or convention for this case.
An implementer using `git log -p` must parse unified diff format, which has edge cases (context lines, no-newline markers) not addressed by the plan.
findings.suggested_fix: For each commit SHA returned by `git log --format=%H -- .github/module-size-limit.txt`, read new_limit via `git show <sha>:.github/module-size-limit.txt` and old_limit via `git show <sha>^:.github/module-size-limit.txt` (catching non-zero exit for the creation case and setting `old_limit=None`). Document this in the plan so the AC's 'old and new limit values' assertion is unambiguous.

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that each git-metrics `compute` function wraps all subprocess/git calls in a try/except (catching at minimum `subprocess.CalledProcessError` and `OSError`) and returns `None` on any such failure, so the c085 registry always receives `None` → `Unavailable` rather than a propagating exception.
evidence: Plan enumerates exactly two None-return conditions: absent source file and empty commit range
No mention of try/except around subprocess.run / git invocations, or of mapping CalledProcessError/OSError → None
The c085 contract converts None → Unavailable, but only if None is actually returned; an exception bypasses that path
findings.finding: The plan specifies `None` return for 'source file absent' and 'no commits in range', but does not specify how subprocess failures from git commands (e.g., git not installed, not a git repo, non-zero exit code, OSError) are handled. An unhandled exception propagating from a metrics `compute` callable would break the registry's evaluation loop rather than yielding `Unavailable`.
findings.location: Approach section — 'each git-metric `compute` returns `None` (never `[]`/`{}`/`0`) when its range yields no commits or the source file is absent'
scenarios: CI runner has a broken git installation — `git log` raises FileNotFoundError, crashing the registry evaluation loop
A metrics call is made outside a git repo (e.g., in a temp directory without .git) — git exits non-zero, raising CalledProcessError
A corrupted object store causes `git show` to fail mid-computation
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach section: 'All git subprocess invocations are wrapped in try/except (CalledProcessError, OSError); any subprocess failure returns None, yielding Unavailable via the registry contract.' Mirror this in the AC as an explicit test case.

findings.finding: The plan does not specify the line-counting semantics for `module_size_distribution`, creating a risk that the metric disagrees with the CI gate. The existing gate and `test_module_size_contract.py` use `text.count('\n')` (newline counting, matching `wc -l`); `len(content.splitlines())` differs for files without a trailing newline. An unspecified method could produce `near_cap_count`/`over_cap_count` values inconsistent with what CI would enforce.
findings.location: Files section — `module_size_distribution`; Approach section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'LOC is counted as `content.count("\n")` to match the CI gate's `wc -l` semantics (mirroring `test_module_size_contract.py`).' Add an AC fixture that includes a file lacking a trailing newline and asserts the expected count.

findings.finding: `refactor_to_addition_ratio` has an unhandled division-by-zero when a commit range contains only deletions (insertions=0). The plan says a 'real churn of 0 returns 0, not None' but doesn't distinguish 'empty range' (return None) from 'deletion-only range' (insertions=0, ratio undefined). A caller passing a pure-refactor/deletion range would get a ZeroDivisionError.
findings.location: Files section — `churn` / `refactor_to_addition_ratio`; Approach section — None/zero semantics
findings.suggested_fix: Return `None` when `total_insertions == 0` (the registry contract already maps `None` → `Unavailable`, which is the right signal for 'ratio undefined'); add a third fixture commit variant — a pure-deletion commit — to the test and assert the function returns `None` for that range.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that all git subprocess calls are wrapped to catch non-zero exit / OSError (git-not-found or not-a-git-repo) and return `None`, so the `Unavailable` contract holds in pip-installed / server-outside-checkout deployments.
evidence: Plan states: 'each git-metric `compute` returns `None` (never `[]`/`{}`/`0`) when its range yields no commits or the source file is absent' — this covers empty-data cases but not git-subprocess-failure cases
Plan states all four functions call git CLI: '`git ls-tree`/`git show` at the ref', '`git log --numstat`', '`git log` over `.github/module-size-limit.txt`'

A pip-installed rebar package (e.g. remote MCP server deployment) would have no `.git` directory at `repo_root`, causing all git subprocesses to fail with 'not a git repository' rather than returning empty results
findings.finding: The plan specifies that each git-metric `compute` returns `None` only when 'its range yields no commits or the source file is absent', but does not specify error handling when the git CLI commands themselves fail (e.g. `repo_root` is not a git repository). When rebar is installed as a Python package (pip install) on a remote MCP server or CI runner without a source checkout, `repo_root` will have no `.git` directory, causing `git ls-tree`, `git log --numstat`, and related subprocess calls to exit non-zero — which would raise an exception rather than returning `None`, breaking the `Unavailable` contract and potentially crashing the metrics pipeline.
findings.location: ## Approach — '`compute` returns `None` (never `[]`/`{}`/`0`) when its range yields no commits or the source file is absent'
scenarios: Remote MCP server shape, server outside the checkout: rebar installed via pip with no source checkout present — all four metric functions throw CalledProcessError/OSError instead of returning None, causing the metrics registry to surface an unhandled exception rather than Unavailable
CI runner shape where rebar is installed as a wheel artifact (not cloned): same failure — git commands error out, metrics crash rather than degrade gracefully
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap each subprocess.run / git invocation in a try/except that catches CalledProcessError and FileNotFoundError (git not on PATH), and returns None in both cases, in addition to the already-specified empty-range None return.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (and test case) for `refactor_to_addition_ratio` when total insertions = 0 (e.g., a pure-deletion range), specifying whether the function returns `None`, `inf`, or raises, so the contract is explicit and tested.
evidence: AC only covers a 'one add-heavy, one delete-heavy' fixture — a positive insertions value is assumed
No AC or test scenario addresses insertions=0 or a pure-deletion range
The Approach section says `compute` returns `None` when 'no data', but a pure-deletion range does have data — it just has 0 insertions, making the ratio undefined
findings.finding: The `refactor_to_addition_ratio` function has no test coverage for the boundary case where total insertions equals zero (division by zero or undefined ratio), which is a real production scenario (e.g., a range containing only deletion commits).
findings.location: Tests section / AC line 2 (`refactor_to_addition_ratio` over a two-commit numstat fixture)
scenarios: A date range that includes only refactoring commits (net deletions, no additions) would cause a ZeroDivisionError or require a sentinel return — neither case is tested or specified
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: '`refactor_to_addition_ratio` over a range with zero insertions returns `None` (not a ZeroDivisionError)' and add a corresponding test case with a pure-deletion fixture commit.

findings.finding: `near_cap_count` and `over_cap_count` are specified as keys in the `module_size_distribution` return dict but neither is asserted in AC1 or any other AC, leaving those fields unverified at acceptance time.
findings.location: AC1 and Files section — `module_size_distribution` return dict
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC1: '… and `near_cap_count` equals the count of fixture modules whose LOC is within 10% of the fixture cap, and `over_cap_count` equals the count of fixture modules exceeding the cap — all exact-value asserts.'

findings.finding: `near_cap_count` and `over_cap_count` are named return-value fields in the Files section's `module_size_distribution` spec but neither field is asserted in any acceptance criterion, leaving two described deliverables without coverage.
findings.location: Files section — `module_size_distribution` return dict `{count, near_cap_count, max_loc, over_cap_count}`; AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the fixture to include one module at 98% of the cap (triggers near_cap_count) and one above the cap (triggers over_cap_count), and add exact-value asserts for both fields in AC1.

findings.finding: The Approach's None/0 distinction (compute returns `None` for no-data vs `0` for genuine zero churn) is a described behavioral contract but has no corresponding acceptance criterion.
findings.location: Approach section — 'each git-metric compute returns None … when its range yields no commits … a real churn of 0 over a populated range returns 0, not None'
findings.suggested_fix: Add two fixture scenarios: (a) a date range before any commits → assert result is None; (b) a commit that touches only non-Python metadata with 0 insertions+deletions → assert result is 0.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the accepted type and format for `since`/`until` across all three range functions (e.g. 'ISO-8601 strings, or any git-accepted revision expression; `None` means unbounded') so an executor can implement a consistent API without guessing.
evidence: Plan states the signatures `churn(repo_root, since, until)` and `cap_change_events(repo_root, since, until)` without specifying parameter types or accepted formats
AC for churn says 'over a two-commit numstat fixture' but doesn't constrain how the range is expressed
No mention of whether None is a valid sentinel for 'all history'
findings.finding: The `since` and `until` parameters for `churn`, `refactor_to_addition_ratio`, and `cap_change_events` have no stated type or format, leaving an executor to guess whether they accept datetime objects, ISO strings, git revision strings (e.g. 'HEAD~30'), or timedelta offsets. Different choices produce incompatible APIs across the three functions and inconsistent test fixture design.
findings.location: Files section — `churn(repo_root, since, until)`, `refactor_to_addition_ratio(repo_root, since, until)`, `cap_change_events(repo_root, since, until)`
scenarios: Executor implements `since`/`until` as datetime objects; downstream consumer passes git revision strings; integration fails silently
Test fixture uses one format while the production registry call uses another, causing the AC to pass in isolation but fail in real use
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Files section: '`since` and `until` accept any git revision expression (commit SHA, tag, or `git log`-compatible date string such as `"2024-01-01"`); passing `None` for either means the range is unbounded in that direction.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extract the `near_cap_count` nearness threshold (currently 10%) into a named constant (e.g. `NEAR_CAP_FRACTION = 0.10`) or a `near_fraction` parameter with a default, so it is configurable without a logic edit.
evidence: Plan describes near_cap_count as 'within 10% of the 800 cap' — a hardcoded percentage, not a configurable parameter
No mention of a named constant, config entry, or function parameter for the nearness threshold
Thresholds defined by policy (how close is 'close'?) are exactly the kind of value that evolves
findings.finding: The `near_cap_count` nearness threshold (10% of cap) is described as baked into the function body rather than exposed as a configurable parameter or named constant. If the team later wants to change what 'near the cap' means, it requires a code edit rather than a config change.
findings.location: Files section — `module_size_distribution` description: 'near_cap_count (within 10% of the 800 cap)'
scenarios: Team decides 15% is a better warning threshold — requires finding and editing the magic number in git_metrics.py
A caller wants a tighter or looser definition of 'near' for a specific dashboard view
findings.suggested_fix: In `module_size_distribution`, define `NEAR_CAP_FRACTION = 0.10` at module level (or accept an optional `near_fraction=0.10` kwarg) and reference it in the near_cap_count calculation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale to the Approach section explaining why the git-subprocess mechanism was chosen (e.g., it matches the established codebase pattern for all git operations, enables querying arbitrary refs — not just HEAD — and keeps the module dependency-free).
evidence: Plan states 'Everything is deterministic and read-only' and 'scans the tree at a ref against the single-sourced cap' — these are properties of the solution, not a rationale for WHY this mechanism was chosen over alternatives.
ABSENCE: no positive rationale explaining why raw git subprocess over the ref (vs. direct filesystem walk or a git library) is the right fit for this codebase.
findings.finding: The plan does not state a positive rationale for choosing the git-subprocess approach over alternatives (e.g., an in-process filesystem walk for the `ref='HEAD'` case, or a git library), which the rubric requires as a plan-quality criterion.
findings.location: Approach section
scenarios: A reviewer or future maintainer cannot understand from the plan why git plumbing was preferred, making the design harder to defend or extend.

findings.finding: The `churn` function is specified in the Files section and Tests section but has no acceptance criterion. An executor implementing it has no formal, verifiable done-state for that function.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section (all four ACs) vs. Files section (`churn(repo_root, since, until)`)
findings.suggested_fix: Add: '- [ ] `churn` over the two-commit numstat fixture returns `{insertions: N, deletions: M}` with values matching the fixture's line counts — asserted exactly.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one AC asserting that `module_size_distribution` (or `cap_change_events`) returns `None` when `.github/module-size-limit.txt` is absent from the fixture repo, validating the None-return contract that the c085 registry depends on.
evidence: Approach: 'each git-metric compute returns None (never []/{}/ 0) when its range yields no commits or the source file is absent'
No AC tests module_size_distribution or cap_change_events when .github/module-size-limit.txt is missing from the fixture repo
This is a specified contract that is unverified
findings.finding: The `None`-return contract when `.github/module-size-limit.txt` is absent — explicitly specified in the Approach section as the sentinel for 'source file absent' — has no corresponding AC or test case asserting this behavior.
findings.location: Approach section ('each git-metric `compute` returns `None` … when its range yields no commits or the source file is absent') / AC section (no corresponding AC exists)
scenarios: A fresh clone or a repo at an early ref before the limit file was committed would trigger this path; the caller (c085 registry) relies on None to emit Unavailable — if the function returns {} or 0 instead, callers silently get wrong data
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'When `.github/module-size-limit.txt` does not exist in the fixture repo, `module_size_distribution` returns `None` (not `{}` or `0`)' and add a corresponding test case using a fixture repo without the limit file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that all git invocations in `git_metrics.py` route through `rebar._store.gitutil.run_git` (the existing shared seam with 10+ call-sites) rather than calling `subprocess.run` directly.
evidence: git_adapter.py line 10: 'built ON TOP of rebar._store.gitutil.run_git — the one shared subprocess.run(["git", …]) shape for the whole codebase'
gitutil.py docstring: 'consolidates the dozen hand-rolled _git() wrappers that had drifted into a different signature/return/error contract each'
grep hits: run_git imported and used in assemble.py, git_adapter.py, repo_snapshot.py, sync.py, push.py, fsck.py, fsck_recover.py, fsck_repair.py, verify_authorship.py — 10+ production call-sites
Plan text makes no mention of run_git anywhere in Files, Approach, or Tests sections
findings.finding: The plan proposes raw git subprocess calls in git_metrics.py (git ls-tree/git show for tree walk, git log --numstat for churn, git log for cap events) without routing through `rebar._store.gitutil.run_git`, the codebase's explicitly documented single shared git subprocess seam. The seam has 10+ active call-sites across production paths (assemble.py, git_adapter.py, repo_snapshot.py, sync.py, push.py, verify_authorship.py, fsck.py, fsck_recover.py, fsck_repair.py, etc.). git_adapter.py's module docstring explicitly states it is 'built ON TOP of rebar._store.gitutil.run_git — the one shared subprocess.run(["git", …]) shape for the whole codebase'. Bypassing it duplicates the subprocess wiring that run_git centralises (no-shell guarantee, consistent timeout/env/text/check API, test-seam mockability).
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py`; Approach section — 'git log --numstat', 'git ls-tree'/'git show'
scenarios: An implementer writes `subprocess.run(["git", "-C", repo_root, "log", "--numstat", …])` directly in git_metrics.py, bypassing the shared seam and making the call impossible to mock via the existing test patterns (monkeypatching gitutil.subprocess.run).
A future change to the git invocation contract (timeout policy, env handling) must now be applied in two places rather than one.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Files section, add: 'All subprocess git calls use `from rebar._store.gitutil import run_git`; no direct `subprocess.run` for git commands.'
